volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: Epidicus (Section 1.1.37 - Section 1.1.85)

sections.label: Section 1.1.37
sections.original: id módo videndum est , ut materies suppetat scutariis ,
sections.translation: Only this must be looked to , that material may be found for the armourers , if in each campaign he yields a spoil to the enemy .

sections.label: Section 1.1.39
sections.translation: Have done now with these matters .

sections.label: Section 1.1.39b
sections.original: Tu ipse , ubi lubet , finem face .
sections.translation: You yourself make an end of them when you please .

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.translation: Cease your enquiries then .

sections.label: Section 1.1.40b
sections.translation: Say , where is Stratippocles himself ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.41
sections.original: Est causa qua causa simul mecum ire veritust .
sections.translation: There is a reason , for which reason he has been afraid to come together with me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.41b
sections.translation: Pray , what is it ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.42
sections.original: Patrem videre se nevolt etiam nunc .
sections.translation: He doesn ’ t wish to see his father as yet .

sections.label: Section 1.1.42b
sections.translation: For what reason ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.42c
sections.translation: You shall hear ; because he has purchased out of the spoil a young female captive of charming and genteel figure .

sections.label: Section 1.1.44b
sections.translation: What is it I hear from you ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.44c
sections.translation: That which I ’ m telling you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.45
sections.translation: Why has he purchased her ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.45b
sections.translation: To please his fancy .

sections.label: Section 1.1.45c
sections.translation: How many fancies has this man ? For assuredly , before he went away from home to the army , he himself commissioned me , that a music-girl whom he was in love with should be purchased of a Procurer for him . That I have managed to accomplish for him .

sections.label: Section 1.1.49
sections.original: Vtcúmque in alto ventust , Epidice , exim velum vortitur .
sections.translation: Whichever way the wind is at sea , Epidicus , in that direction the sail is shifted .

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: Vaé misero mihí , male perdidit me .
sections.translation: Woe unto wretched me ! He has utterly undone me !

sections.label: Section 1.1.50b
sections.translation: What ’ s the meaning of this ? What ’ s the matter , pray ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.51
sections.original: Quid istanc quam emit , quanti eam emit ?
sections.translation: Well now — she whom he has bought , at what sum has he purchased her ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.51b
sections.translation: A very little .

sections.label: Section 1.1.51c
sections.translation: That I don ’ t ask you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.52
sections.translation: What then ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.52b
sections.translation: For how many minae ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.52c
sections.translation: For so many .

sections.label: Section 1.1.53
sections.original: íd adeo argentum ab danista apud Thébas sumpsit faenore ,
sections.translation: For that purpose , he borrowed the money on interest of a Banker at Thebes , at a didrachm for each silver mina per day .

sections.label: Section 1.1.54b
sections.translation: Surprising !

sections.label: Section 1.1.55
sections.original: Ét is danista advenit una cúm eo , qui argentum petit .
sections.translation: This Banker , too , has come together with him , and is dunning for his money .

sections.label: Section 1.1.56
sections.original: Di immortales , ut ego interii basilice .
sections.translation: Immortal Gods ! now I ’ m fairly done for !

sections.label: Section 1.1.56b
sections.translation: Why so , or . what ’ s the matter , Epidicus ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.57b
sections.translation: He has proved my ruin !

sections.label: Section 1.1.57c
sections.translation: Who ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.57d
sections.translation: Who ? He who lost his arms .

sections.label: Section 1.1.58
sections.translation: But why so ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.58b
sections.original: Quia cottidie ipse ad me ab legione epistulas
sections.translation: Because he himself was every day sending me letters from the army — but I shall hold my tongue ; it ’ s best to do so .

sections.label: Section 1.1.60
sections.original: plus scire satiust quam loqui servom hominem . ea sapientia est .
sections.translation: It ’ s best for a man in servitude to know more than he says ; that ’ s true wisdom .

sections.label: Section 1.1.61
sections.original: Néscio edepol quid tu timidus es , trepidas , Epidice , ita voltum tuom †
sections.translation: On my faith , I don ’ t understand why you are alarmed . You are frightened , Epidicus ; I see it by your countenance . You seem here , in my absence , to have got into some scrape or other .

sections.label: Section 1.1.63
sections.translation: Can ’ t you cease annoying me ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.63b
sections.translation: I ’ ll be off .

sections.label: Section 1.1.63c
sections.translation: Stand still ; I ’ ll not let you go from here .

sections.label: Section 1.1.64
sections.translation: Why do you hold me back ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.64b
sections.original: Amatne ístam quam emit de praeda ?
sections.translation: Is he in love with her whom he has purchased out of the spoil ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.64c
sections.translation: Do you ask me ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.65
sections.translation: He dotes to death upon her .

sections.label: Section 1.1.65b
sections.translation: The hide will be stripped from off my back .

sections.label: Section 1.1.66
sections.original: Plusque amat quam te umquam amavit .
sections.translation: He loves her , too , more than ever he loved you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.66b
sections.translation: May Jupiter confound you !

sections.label: Section 1.1.67
sections.original: Mítte nunciam , nam ílle me vetuit domum
sections.translation: Let me go now ; for he has forbidden me to go to our house ; he ordered me to come here ( pointing to the house ) to our neighbour ’ s , Chaeribulus ; there he bade me wait ; he ’ s about to come there himself .

sections.label: Section 1.1.69b
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.69c
sections.translation: I ’ ll tell you ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.original: quía patrem prius convenire se non volt neque conspicari ,
sections.translation: because he doesn ’ t wish to meet with or see his father , before he has paid down this money which is owing for her .

sections.label: Section 1.1.72
sections.translation: O dear ! an involved business , i ’ faith .

sections.label: Section 1.1.72b
sections.translation: Do let go of me , that I may now be off forthwith .

sections.label: Section 1.1.73
sections.translation: When the old gentleman knows this , our ship will fairly founder .

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.translation: What matters it to me in what way you come to your end ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.77
sections.original: Quía perire solus nolo , te cupio perire mecum ,
sections.translation: Because I don ’ t wish to perish alone ; I ’ d like you to perish with me , well-wisher with well-wisher .

sections.label: Section 1.1.78b
sections.original: Abi ín malam rem maxumam a me
sections.translation: Away with you from me to utter and extreme perdition with those terms of yours !

sections.label: Section 1.1.79b
sections.translation: Be off , then , if you are in great haste about anything .

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: Numquam hominem quemquam conveni , unde abierim lubentius .—
sections.translation: I never met with any person from whom I parted with greater pleasure .

sections.label: Section 1.1.81
sections.original: Illic hinc abiit . solus nunc es . quo in loco haec res sit vides ,
sections.translation: He ’ s gone away from here ; you are now alone . In what plight this matter is , you now see , Epidicus . Unless you have some resources in your own self , you are done for . Ruination so great is impending over you — unless you support yourself stoutly , you cannot hold up ; to such a degree are mountains of misfortune threatening to tumble on you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.translation: Neither does any plan just now please me by means of which to find myself disengaged from my entanglement . To my misfortune , by my trickeries I have forced the old man to imagine that he was making purchase of his own daughter ; whereas he has bought for his own son
